text.title: Crash-Pipeline-as-Code Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that prevent the recurring "we're missing symbols for the build that crashed in production" failure.

rule.statement: Symbol upload (dSYMs for iOS, ProGuard/R8 for Android, source maps for web, debug info for native) MUST happen in CI as part of the release job — never from a developer laptop. The upload step is committed in the pipeline file and runs on every release tag. Manual upload is forbidden.
rule.rationale: CI is the only path that runs every time. Developer laptops produce 95% reliability; releases need 100%.

rule.statement: After upload, the CI MUST verify deobfuscation works by calling the vendor's API to check the symbol was indexed AND running a synthetic crash test (or test event) that returns symbolicated frames. A release that does not verify symbols is rolled back, not shipped.

rationale.source: Sentry, Crashlytics best-practice docs + post-incident reviews

rule.rationale: Symbol upload returns 200 but vendor-side processing can silently fail. Without verification, the team learns about the failure when a real crash arrives un-symbolicated and the alert is useless.

rule.statement: Alert rules MUST be defined in version-controlled code (Terraform, vendor YAML config, or vendor CLI scripts). Web-console-only rules are forbidden — they vanish in audit, drift silently, and have no review history. If the vendor has no IaC: write a script that exports rules as JSON, commit, and run a daily drift-check.
rule.rationale: Console rules are mutable by anyone with admin and have zero audit trail. Code rules have PR reviews, history, and rollback.

rule.statement: The primary "is the app healthy" dashboard MUST be defined in code (vendor's dashboard JSON / Terraform / Grafana JSON). Console-built dashboards are allowed for exploration but not as the team's source of truth. Dashboard ownership is documented (owner field, on-call rotation).
rule.rationale: Dashboards owned by people who leave evaporate. Dashboards in code survive personnel change and can be cloned per service.

rule.statement: Retention settings (events, attachments, releases) MUST be set in code, not console defaults. Default retention is recorded with the reason (cost vs investigation window). PII redaction settings are also committed; sending PII to the vendor is a contract issue, not a config detail.
rule.rationale: Default retention may exceed what GDPR/HIPAA permits for crash data. PII leakage to vendor is a controller-processor contract breach. Both must be reviewable in code.

example.title: CI step that obeys r1 + r2
example.code:
- name: Upload iOS dSYMs to Sentry run: | sentry-cli upload-dif \ --org $SENTRY_ORG --project $SENTRY_PROJECT \ ios-build/*.dSYM/dSYMs/
- name: Verify dSYM indexed (synthetic event) run: | EVENT_ID=$(sentry-cli send-event --release $RELEASE --message "ci-verify-$BUILD") sleep 10 sentry-cli events get $EVENT_ID --json | jq -e '.entries[]|select(.type=="exception")|.data.values[0].stacktrace.frames[0].function != null'
example.note: Upload + verify in CI. Synthetic event confirms deobfuscation actually works for this release.

example.title: Pre-methodology baseline
example.code: "After every release, run /Scripts/upload_dsyms.sh on Marek's MacBook. Marek is on PTO this week. Pawel will do it. The script path may have changed."
example.note: Single point of failure, no verification, no audit trail. Production crashes 3 days later have no symbols.
